title: The Changing Economic World: The UK Economy
examTip: The UK is a post-industrial HIC — services dominate (80% of GDP). Always link the north-south divide to deindustrialisation, and evaluate government responses with evidence of their limitations.
introTips: Know the four sectors: primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary — and the UK's shift towards tertiary/quaternary.
The north-south divide is real and measurable — always quote specific figures (GDP, house prices, life expectancy).
Evaluating Northern Powerhouse: HS2's Birmingham-Manchester leg was cancelled in 2023 — use this.
intro: The UK's economy has gone through one of the biggest transformations of any country in the 20th century — from 'workshop of the world' to a service-dominated, globalised economy where financial services and creative industries matter more than steel and coal. But this transformation has left deep regional scars, and whether the government can really 'level up' those left behind is one of the most debated questions in British geography.

keyPoints.heading: The UK as a post-industrial HIC
keyPoints.content: The UK has a GNI per capita of approximately $47,000 and is a member of the G7 (the world's seven largest advanced economies). Its economic structure has fundamentally shifted: the primary sector (agriculture, mining) now contributes less than 2% of GDP; the secondary sector (manufacturing) around 10%; the tertiary sector (services — retail, healthcare, education, finance) around 80%; and the quaternary sector (research and development, IT, media, finance) is the fastest growing. Key sectors include: financial services (City of London contributes about 7% of UK GDP), creative industries (music, gaming, film, design), professional services (law, accountancy), and tourism (around 35 million overseas visitors per year, before COVID-19). This makes the UK a post-industrial economy.
writeBullets: GNI per capita ~$47,000 — G7 member; highly developed economy
Primary sector <2% of GDP — farming and mining are marginal
Secondary sector ~10% — manufacturing much reduced since deindustrialisation
Tertiary sector ~80% — services dominate; retail, healthcare, finance, education
Quaternary sector — research, IT, media, finance; fastest growing
City of London — financial services ~7% of GDP; major global hub
Tourism — ~35m overseas visitors per year; major contributor to economy
keyPoints.memTip: PSTQ — Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Quaternary. In the UK: tiny, small, huge, growing.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget the quaternary sector — it's the knowledge economy and it's what's growing fastest in the UK.

keyPoints.heading: Deindustrialisation and its legacy
keyPoints.content: Traditional UK industries declined rapidly from the 1970s to 1990s. Coal mining was devastated — the miners' strike of 1984–85 ended in defeat, and pit closures followed; former coalfield communities like those in South Yorkshire and South Wales still struggle economically. Steel: Sheffield once employed 100,000 steelworkers; fewer than 10,000 remain. Shipbuilding on the Clyde (Glasgow) and Tyne (Newcastle) has almost entirely gone. Textiles: Lancashire and Yorkshire's mill towns have been economically transformed — or left behind. Causes of deindustrialisation included globalisation (cheaper manufacturing in Asia), mechanisation (automation reduced the need for workers), and political choices (Thatcher's market-oriented policies did not protect declining industries). The legacy is regional deprivation in former industrial areas — sometimes called 'rust belt' towns. Meanwhile, a new economy grew: science parks, knowledge economy, media — largely concentrated in the south of England.
writeBullets: Coal — miners' strike 1984–85; pit closures devastated Yorkshire, Welsh valleys
Steel — Sheffield: 100,000 steelworkers (1971) vs <10,000 today
Shipbuilding — Clyde and Tyne yards almost entirely closed
Textiles — Lancashire and Yorkshire mill towns transformed or abandoned
Causes — globalisation (cheaper Asian labour), mechanisation, Thatcher's policies
Legacy — regional deprivation in former industrial 'rust belt' towns
New economy — science parks, Cambridge/Oxford clusters, media — grew mainly in south
keyPoints.memTip: Coal, Steel, Ships, Textiles — the 'Big Four' that deindustrialisation destroyed in the UK.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Sheffield's steel statistics (100,000 to <10,000) are a powerful specific fact — use it.

keyPoints.heading: The north-south divide
keyPoints.content: The north-south divide is a persistent economic and social divide between southern England (especially London and the South East) and the north, Midlands, Wales and Northern Ireland. GDP per capita in south-east England is approximately twice that of north-east England. House prices: average London property ~£500,000 vs Burnley ~£100,000. Life expectancy for men: Kensington (London) 83 years vs Blackpool 74 years — a 9-year gap. Employment rates and graduate retention are both significantly higher in the south. Government responses: the Northern Powerhouse (George Osborne, 2014) aimed to boost northern cities through HS2 (high-speed rail), HS3 (connecting northern cities), and devolution (Greater Manchester Mayor). The Levelling Up agenda (2022 White Paper) promised investment in deprived areas. However, the Birmingham-to-Manchester leg of HS2 was cancelled in 2023 due to escalating costs, undermining the Northern Powerhouse ambition. Many geographers argue the north-south divide is widening despite policy.
writeBullets: GDP per capita — SE England ~2× that of NE England
House prices — London average ~£500,000 vs Burnley ~£100,000
Life expectancy — Kensington men: 83 years vs Blackpool: 74 years (9-year gap)
Northern Powerhouse — George Osborne 2014; HS2, HS3, devolution to Manchester Mayor
Levelling Up White Paper 2022 — promised investment in deprived areas
HS2 Birmingham-Manchester cancelled 2023 — major blow to Northern Powerhouse
Critics argue north-south divide is widening despite government policy
keyPoints.memTip: North lags, south leads — in GDP, house prices, life expectancy. Same country, different worlds.
keyPoints.tutor_note: HS2's cancellation in 2023 is important and current — it's excellent evaluation material for north-south divide questions.

keyPoints.heading: The UK in globalisation and rural challenges
keyPoints.content: The UK is a major global trading nation, exporting over £700 billion of goods and services per year. London is one of the world's top two or three financial centres (alongside New York and Tokyo). The UK attracts significant FDI (Foreign Direct Investment): the Nissan factory in Sunderland and Toyota in Burnaston are important examples of manufacturing FDI. The UK left the EU Single Market in 2020 (Brexit), introducing new trade barriers with the UK's largest trading partner. The UK is now negotiating new trade deals globally. Science and technology: Cambridge Science Park (90+ companies), Silicon Fen (tech cluster around Cambridge), and Manchester's digital economy are growing. Rural UK faces its own challenges: depopulation as young people leave for cities; withdrawal of services (post offices, GP surgeries, bus routes); the second home problem inflates house prices in areas like Cornwall, the Lake District and Yorkshire Dales, pricing out local residents. Rural opportunities include tourism, farm diversification, and renewable energy (wind farms).
writeBullets: UK exports £700bn+/year — major global trading nation
London — top global financial centre alongside New York and Tokyo
FDI — Nissan (Sunderland), Toyota (Burnaston) — significant manufacturing investment
Brexit 2020 — left EU Single Market; new trade barriers with largest trading partner
Cambridge Science Park / Silicon Fen — knowledge economy hub; 90+ companies
Rural challenges — depopulation, service withdrawal, second home prices (Cornwall, Lakes)
Rural opportunities — tourism, farm diversification, wind farms
keyPoints.memTip: Global in, local out — UK attracts global investment but some rural areas lose people and services.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Rural challenges are often underweighted by students — make sure to know at least two challenges and two opportunities.

tables.title: North-South divide: comparing key indicators
rows: Lower — approximately half of SE England
Many graduates leave for southern cities
Strong graduate retention in London, Cambridge

1.question: What happened to Sheffield's steel industry between 1971 and today, and why?
options: Employment fell from 100,000 to fewer than 10,000 steelworkers — due to globalisation (cheaper Asian steel), mechanisation and political choices
Employment grew from 10,000 to 100,000 — Sheffield attracted new steel investment from China
The steel industry was nationalised in 1971 and all workers were employed by the government
Sheffield switched from steel to electronics manufacturing, retaining all its workforce
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Sheffield's steel industry collapsed — from 100,000 workers in 1971 to fewer than 10,000 today — because of globalisation (cheaper steel from Asia), mechanisation (fewer workers needed) and political factors.
1.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Sheffield's steel industry collapsed dramatically — from 100,000 workers (1971) to fewer than 10,000 today — as a result of globalisation, mechanisation and deindustrialisation.

2.question: Which specific evidence best illustrates the north-south divide in life expectancy?
options: Men in Kensington (London) live to 83 years on average, compared to 74 years in Blackpool — a 9-year gap
Women in Birmingham live longer than women in Manchester due to better NHS funding
Life expectancy in Scotland is 10 years lower than in Wales due to higher alcohol consumption
The average UK life expectancy has increased to 82 years, closing the gap between north and south
2.correctFeedback: Perfect. Kensington (83 years) vs Blackpool (74 years) — a 9-year gap in male life expectancy within the same country is striking evidence of the north-south divide in health outcomes.
2.wrongFeedback: That's not the right comparison. The clearest example is Kensington men (83 years) vs Blackpool men (74 years) — a 9-year gap showing how geography within the UK shapes how long you live.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the quaternary sector — it's the knowledge and research economy (IT, finance, media, R&D) and it's what's growing fastest in the UK; students often only list three sectors.
Stating HS2 is still being built between Birmingham and Manchester — the northern leg was cancelled in October 2023; only London to Birmingham remains planned.
Describing the north-south divide as only about income — it's also about house prices, life expectancy, employment rates and access to services; always use multiple measures.
Saying Brexit had no impact on trade — it introduced new trade barriers (customs checks, regulatory differences) with the EU, the UK's largest trading partner.
Confusing FDI (foreign direct investment — e.g., Nissan factory) with aid — FDI is profit-motivated business investment, not charity or government assistance.
examTips: For the north-south divide, always quote at least two different types of evidence — income AND life expectancy, or house prices AND unemployment — to show you understand it is multi-dimensional.
When evaluating Northern Powerhouse or Levelling Up, mention that the Manchester leg of HS2 was cancelled in 2023 — this shows your knowledge is current and you can evaluate policy effectiveness.
Rural questions often ask for challenges AND opportunities — depopulation and second homes (challenges) vs tourism, renewable energy and farm diversification (opportunities). Give both.
Sector shift questions: remember the UK is not de-industrialised — manufacturing still exists (~10% of GDP). The UK has diversified, not abandoned industry entirely.
revisionCardBullets: UK GNI per capita ~$47,000 — G7 member; post-industrial HIC
Economic sectors: primary (<2%), secondary (~10%), tertiary (~80%), quaternary (growing)
City of London — financial services ~7% of GDP; global financial hub
Deindustrialisation — coal, steel, shipbuilding, textiles collapsed from 1970s
North-south divide: SE GDP ~2× NE; London house £500k vs Burnley £100k
Life expectancy: Kensington men 83 yrs vs Blackpool 74 yrs — 9-year gap
Northern Powerhouse (2014) — HS2, devolution; Manchester HS2 leg cancelled 2023
Levelling Up White Paper 2022 — investment in deprived areas
FDI — Nissan Sunderland, Toyota Burnaston; UK major destination for investment
Brexit 2020 — left EU Single Market; new trade barriers
Rural challenges: depopulation, service withdrawal, second home prices (Cornwall, Lakes)
